title: The Foundation of a Generalized Parallel Connection
speaker: Zach Walsh
abstract: The foundation of a matroid is an algebraic invariant that controls representations over any partial field, hyperfield, or more generally, any pasture. We show that, under certain conditions, the foundation of a generalized parallel connection of two matroids is the relative tensor product of their foundations. We use this to show that the foundation of a 2-sum of two matroids is the absolute tensor product of their foundations. This is joint work with Matt Baker, Oliver Lorscheid, and Tianyi Zhang.

proof_stages.label: Pastures and Grassmann-Pluecker matroid representations
proof_stages.summary: The talk recasts matroid representations as determinant-like functions satisfying support, sign, and three-term null relations, then defines pastures as coefficient systems broad enough to include fields, partial fields, and hyperfields.

proof_stages.label: Universal foundations of matroids
proof_stages.summary: Using morphisms and tensor-like categorical operations on pastures, the foundation F(M) is introduced as the pasture representing rescaling classes of P-representations of a matroid over every pasture P.

proof_stages.label: Generalized parallel connection as matroid gluing
proof_stages.summary: The generalized parallel connection P_T(M_1,M_2) is defined by gluing two matroids along a common set T that is a modular flat on one side, and its special cases are identified as direct sum, parallel connection, 2-sum, and delta-Y exchange.

proof_stages.label: Naive relative tensor-product formula and its failure
proof_stages.summary: The natural candidate formula F(P_T(M_1,M_2)) = F(M_1) tensor_{F(T)} F(M_2) is proposed via the canonical maps from the common restriction, but a computed counterexample shows it is not always an isomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Modular-on-both-sides foundation theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main positive theorem states that if the common set T is a modular flat of both matroids, then the relative tensor-product formula for the foundation is valid, proved by an explicit functorial bijection of rescaling classes of representations.

proof_stages.label: Direct sums, parallel connections, and 2-sums
proof_stages.summary: The theorem and an additional deletion argument yield tensor-product formulas for direct sums and parallel connections, extend to 2-sums, and imply that any non-3-connected matroid has a foundation decomposing as a tensor product.

proof_stages.label: Delta-Y preservation and excluded-minor consequences
proof_stages.summary: Further results show that delta-Y exchange, and more generally segment-cosegment exchange, preserves the foundation, leading to preservation statements for excluded minors over an arbitrary pasture.

proof_stages.label: Open problems on gluing and connectivity
proof_stages.summary: The talk ends by asking when the general relative tensor-product map is an isomorphism and whether sufficiently high connectivity prevents a matroid foundation from decomposing as a tensor product.
